At $127.23, City Holding is trading on a P/E of 14.1x, which screens as expensive relative to both its own fair P/E estimate and the wider US banks peer group.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share and is a common way investors judge how much they are paying for a bank's current profit stream. A higher P/E often reflects confidence in the stability of earnings or expectations for future growth, while a lower P/E can point to more muted expectations or higher perceived risk.

For City Holding, the valuation signals are mixed. On one hand, the stock is described as trading at a 38.8% discount to an intrinsic value estimate based on future cash flows. On the other hand, the current P/E of 14.1x is materially above an estimated fair P/E of 9.5x, a level the market could potentially rotate toward if sentiment cools or growth expectations remain modest.

The condor (their 390 lb capacity drone) has been taken out of limbo and has been tested for flight safety over the years. They failed a drop test which is meant for human-carrying aircraft, not drones, other than that they were good to go as far as flight tests and safety. It is suspected that this was done to slow down the development of heavy drones since there were no rules to govern them. On the same date November 4th they will be loosening the restrictions and releasing the long awaited heavy drone rules. Currently the company has at least two potential customers waiting for this to happen.
